title: Canada's Prime Minister Stands Up to Trump—and It's Working
summary:
What would you do if the biggest kid on the block kept threatening to take your lunch money? Canada's Prime Minister Mark Carney faced that problem when Donald Trump became U.S. president again. Trump slapped tariffs (taxes on goods from other countries) on Canadian products and even joked about making Canada the 51st state. But Carney didn't panic. Instead, he got busy making new friends.

For years, Canada sent almost 80% of its exports to the U.S. and often followed America's lead on world issues. But with Trump being so unpredictable, Carney decided Canada needed other partners. He reached out to countries like India, China, Japan, South Korea, and Australia. He even invited India's Prime Minister Narendra Modi to a big meeting in Canada to fix a broken friendship. He also worked to get closer to Europe.

Experts say Carney has been "standing strong and resolute"—that means he hasn't backed down, even when people at home criticized him. One expert, Brett House, said Carney was smart not to rush into a bad trade deal with the U.S. like some other countries did. "Commitments from this president are absolutely worthless," House said.

Carney calls himself a pragmatist—someone who focuses on what works, not just big ideas. He talks mostly about the economy and trade, and avoids other tricky topics. So far, Canadians seem to like it. A recent poll shows 58% of Canadians approve of him, up 10% from a year ago. But not everyone is happy. Some worry that his big projects to build things faster might ignore the rights of Indigenous communities whose land could be affected. Still, after a year of Trump's chaos, many Canadians are glad their leader is standing tall.
why_it_matters: This shows how smaller countries can handle big bullies in trade, and it might affect the prices of things your family buys that come from Canada, like maple syrup or hockey gear.

keywords.explanation: Extra taxes a country puts on goods coming from another country.
Products a country sells to other countries.
Someone who makes decisions based on what actually works, not just ideas.
Very determined and not giving up easily.
People who are the first to live in a land, like Native Americans or First Nations in Canada.
A survey that asks people their opinions to see what the public thinks.

questions.question: What did President Trump do to Canadian products?
options: He made them illegal to sell.
He doubled their price only in Canada.

questions.question: Who is the Prime Minister of Canada in this story?
What did Carney do to counter Trump’s threats?
options: He sent soldiers to the U.S. border.
He reached out to other countries like India and Japan.
He stopped all trade with the U.S. immediately.
He agreed to make Canada the 51st state.
questions.correct_answer: He reached out to other countries like India and Japan.

questions.question: What did an expert say about Trump’s commitments?
How many Canadians approve of Carney, according to the poll?
What do some people worry Carney’s building projects might harm?

background_read: Canada and the United States are neighbors and have traded with each other for a very long time.
President Donald Trump (the U.S. president since 2025) put tariffs on Canadian goods and even joked about making Canada the 51st state.
Prime Minister Mark Carney (Canada’s leader) decided to make new trade friends around the world instead of depending too much on the U.S.
Article_Structure: WHO: Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney.
WHAT: He is standing up to U.S. President Trump’s threats and tariffs by building closer ties with countries like India, China, and Europe.
WHERE: In Canada and during international meetings.
WHY: Because Trump’s unpredictable actions made Canada’s old trade relationship uncertain, and Carney thinks Canada needs other partners to stay safe.

perspectives.description: They are glad he is showing strength and not rushing into a bad deal, and they like that he focuses on the economy.
Some worry that his big projects to build faster could ignore the rights of Indigenous peoples whose land might be used.
They see a major shift in Canada’s friendships, moving away from the U.S. and toward Asia and Europe, which could change world trade for years.
